case_name: Suman Lata And Ors. vs Madan Mohan Sonkar And Ors. on 17 November, 2005
court: High Court of Judicature at Allahabad

compensation_breakdown.claimant_or_victim: Dependents of Dr. Kamlesh Kumar (widow, children, father)
compensation_breakdown.tribunal_amount: Rs. 16,09,000/- (Rs. 15,92,000 pecuniary loss + Rs. 15,000 loss of consortium + Rs. 2,000 funeral expenses)
compensation_breakdown.interest: 7.5% p.a. from date of filing of petition till actual payment
compensation_breakdown.notes: High Court confirmed the total award but reduced interest from 9% to 7.5% and ordered minors' share to be deposited in a nationalized bank/government security.

facts: Dr. Kamlesh Kumar, an eye surgeon aged about 43, while riding his scooter (No. U.P. 42/1395) was knocked down from behind by a jeep (Reg. No. P. 42-A 0174) and was declared brought dead. The jeep was insured with New India Assurance Co. Ltd.; driver Gaya Prasad had a valid driving licence and the owner was Madan Mohan Sonkar. The widow, children and father filed a claim alleging rash and negligent driving; the Tribunal awarded Rs. 16,09,000 with 9% interest. Both the insurer and the claimants filed first appeals; the High Court largely upheld the award but reduced interest and directed investment safeguards for minors.
arguments_claimant: Claimants submitted that the deceased's revised pay scale (w.e.f. 1.1.1996) fixed his salary at Rs. 12,593 and that future prospects and promotion warranted a higher multiplicand (relying on Susamma Thomas and Sarla Dixit), asking for higher dependency (claimed up to Rs. 25,000). They argued no one-third deduction for personal expenses should be made and that the Tribunal's adoption of Susamma Thomas principles was correct.
arguments_respondent: The New India Assurance Company contended the Tribunal applied a wrong formula, that the deceased's basic salary was Rs. 9,776 and that one-third deduction for personal expenses should have been made; it also pleaded contributory negligence (though no material supported it). The insurer challenged the award's legality and quantum and relied on limitations on insurer appeals absent permission under Section 170 and on the grounds under Section 149(2) to avoid liability.
ratio_decidendi: "The compensation awarded must be just, fair and reasonable." The Court held that the Tribunal's adoption of the principles in Susamma Thomas to assess full and just compensation was appropriate and that where facts establish the jeep hit the scooterist from behind the doctrine of "res ipsa loquitur" applies, negating contributory negligence. The Court further held that a formal deduction of one-third for personal expenses was not required here because "the Tribunal while applying aforesaid formula of the case of Susamma Thomas straightaway fixed the amount of compensation at 9,776 x 1400/1032=13262 per month instead of calculating it on the principle of 9,776 X2000/1032," so the Tribunal effectively made a larger deduction. Finally, the Court emphasised the limited scope for appellate interference in quantum (and noted the insurer's restricted remedies under Sections 146/147/149/170), finding no substantial error to justify altering the Tribunal's award except for reducing the rate of interest.
final_order: Both appeals were dismissed except that the rate of interest was reduced from 9% p.a. to 7.5% p.a.; the amount awarded (Rs. 16,09,000) was to stand, minors' shares to be deposited in a nationalized bank/government security initially for three years (renewable) and interest thereon to be used for maintenance; no order as to costs.
summary: This is a motor-accident death claim: Dr. Kamlesh Kumar was killed when a jeep struck his scooter. The Claims Tribunal awarded Rs. 16,09,000 to his dependents; the New India Assurance Company appealed against liability/quantum and the claimants sought enhancement. The High Court upheld the Tribunal's finding of negligence, rejected contributory negligence, retained the award as just and reasonable (applying Susamma Thomas principles), reduced interest to 7.5%, and ordered safeguards for minors' shares.
embedding_summary: Suman Lata & Ors. v. Madan Mohan Sonkar & Ors., High Court of Allahabad, judgment 17 November 2005 (I(2007)ACC209; AIR 2006 (NOC) 1505 (ALL)). Facts: death of Dr. Kamlesh Kumar (43) on 12.08.1996 after his scooter (U.P. 42/1395) was hit from behind by a jeep (P.42-A 0174); jeep insured by New India Assurance Co. Ltd.; owner Madan Mohan Sonkar; driver Gaya Prasad held valid licence. Key issues: negligence, contributory negligence, quantum of compensation (use of Susamma Thomas and Sarla Dixit principles, multiplier, deduction of one-third), insurer's ability to challenge quantum (Sections 146/147/149/170 MV Act), and interest rate. Outcome: Tribunal award of Rs. 16,09,000 confirmed (Rs. 15,92,000 pecuniary + Rs. 15,000 consortium + Rs. 2,000 funeral), interest reduced to 7.5% p.a., directions to deposit minors' shares. Authorities relied on include Susamma Thomas, Sarla Dixit, Helen C. Rebello, Chinnama George, New India Assurance v. Charlie, and others; statutory references include Sections 163A, 168, 170, 149 and related provisions of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988.
legal_principles: Compensation must be "just, fair and reasonable"; Tribunal has wide but judicial discretion ('which to it appears to be just').
Where facts are self-evident the doctrine of 'res ipsa loquitur' applies and contributory negligence is negated.
Insurer's right to appeal on quantum is limited; if insurer cannot invoke Section 149(2) to avoid liability it may be barred from challenging the award (permission under Section 170 implicated).
A formal one-third deduction for personal expenses is not mandatory where the Tribunal's method of fixing dependency effectively accounts for such deduction.
Schedule II/Section 163A multipliers guide but Tribunal's adoption of established precedents (Susamma Thomas, Sarla Dixit) to assess future prospects and quantum is permissible.
Tribunals should protect minors' compensation by ordering deposit/investment of awarded sums to prevent exploitation or frittering away.
sections_cited: Section 149 Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 (including sub-section (2) and (7))
cases_cited: General Manager, State Road Transport Corporation, Kerala v. Susamma Thomas (Mrs.) and Ors.
Smt. Sarla Dixit and Anr. v. Balwant Yadav
Divisional Controller, K.S.R.T.C. v. Mahadeva Shetty
Helen C. Rebello v. Maharashtra State Road Transport Corporation
Asha and Ors. v. United India Insurance Co. Ltd. and Anr.
Chinnama George and Ors. v. N.K. Raju and Anr.
New India Assurance Co. Ltd. v. Smt. Kiran Singh
New India Assurance Co. Ltd. v. Charlie
Tamil Nadu State Road Transport Corporation Ltd. v. Sekar Raj Priya
Fair v. London and North Western Railway Co. (1869) 21 LT 326

bm25_text: Suman Lata And Ors. vs Madan Mohan Sonkar And Ors. on 17 November, 2005 High Court of Judicature at Allahabad 2005 Dr. Kamlesh Kumar, an eye surgeon aged about 43, while riding his scooter (No. U.P. 42/1395) was knocked down from behind by a jeep (Reg. No. P. 42-A 0174) and was declared brought dead. The jeep was insured with New India Assurance Co. Ltd.; driver Gaya Prasad had a valid driving licence and the owner was Madan Mohan Sonkar. The widow, children and father filed a claim alleging rash and negligent driving; the Tribunal awarded Rs. 16,09,000 with 9% interest. Both the insurer and the claimants filed first appeals; the High Court largely upheld the award but reduced interest and directed investment safeguards for minors. Claimants submitted that the deceased's revised pay scale (w.e.f. 1.1.1996) fixed his salary at Rs. 12,593 and that future prospects and promotion warranted a higher multiplicand (relying on Susamma Thomas and Sarla Dixit), asking for higher dependency (claimed up to Rs. 25,000). They argued no one-third deduction for personal expenses should be made and that the Tribunal's adoption of Susamma Thomas principles was correct. The New India Assurance Company contended the Tribunal applied a wrong formula, that the deceased's basic salary was Rs. 9,776 and that one-third deduction for personal expenses should have been made; it also pleaded contributory negligence (though no material supported it). The insurer challenged the award's legality and quantum and relied on limitations on insurer appeals absent permission under Section 170 and on the grounds under Section 149(2) to avoid liability. "The compensation awarded must be just, fair and reasonable." The Court held that the Tribunal's adoption of the principles in Susamma Thomas to assess full and just compensation was appropriate and that where facts establish the jeep hit the scooterist from behind the doctrine of "res ipsa loquitur" applies, negating contributory negligence. The Court further held that a formal deduction of one-third for personal expenses was not required here because "the Tribunal while applying aforesaid formula of the case of Susamma Thomas straightaway fixed the amount of compensation at 9,776 x 1400/1032=13262 per month instead of calculating it on the principle of 9,776 X2000/1032," so the Tribunal effectively made a larger deduction. Finally, the Court emphasised the limited scope for appellate interference in quantum (and noted the insurer's restricted remedies under Sections 146/147/149/170), finding no substantial error to justify altering the Tribunal's award except for reducing the rate of interest. Both appeals were dismissed except that the rate of interest was reduced from 9% p.a. to 7.5% p.a.; the amount awarded (Rs. 16,09,000) was to stand, minors' shares to be deposited in a nationalized bank/government security initially for three years (renewable) and interest thereon to be used for maintenance; no order as to costs. This is a motor-accident death claim: Dr. Kamlesh Kumar was killed when a jeep struck his scooter. The Claims Tribunal awarded Rs. 16,09,000 to his dependents; the New India Assurance Company appealed against liability/quantum and the claimants sought enhancement. The High Court upheld the Tribunal's finding of negligence, rejected contributory negligence, retained the award as just and reasonable (applying Susamma Thomas principles), reduced interest to 7.5%, and ordered safeguards for minors' shares. Ltd.; owner Madan Mohan Sonkar; driver Gaya Prasad held valid licence. Key issues: negligence, contributory negligence, quantum of compensation (use of Susamma Thomas and Sarla Dixit principles, multiplier, deduction of one-third), insurer's ability to challenge quantum (Sections 146/147/149/170 MV Act), and interest rate. Outcome: Tribunal award of Rs. 16,09,000 confirmed (Rs. 15,92,000 pecuniary + Rs. 15,000 consortium + Rs. 2,000 funeral), interest reduced to 7.5% p.a., directions to deposit minors' shares.
